does: This paper introduces Stable-GFlowNet, a modified approach to generative flow networks designed for adversarial red-teaming of large language models. The core contribution removes the challenging partition function Z estimation that destabilizes standard GFN training, replacing it with pairwise comparisons between trajectories. The method incorporates robust masking against noisy reward signals and adds a fluency stabilizer to prevent mode collapse into gibberish outputs. The authors demonstrate that S-GFN maintains GFN's optimal policy properties while substantially improving training stability and enabling discovery of both effective and diverse adversarial prompts across various LLM red-teaming settings.
connects: This work builds on recent applications of generative flow networks to the challenge of finding diverse adversarial examples for LLMs. GFNs have emerged as promising tools for distribution matching and mode coverage, but their notorious training instability and propensity for mode collapse have limited practical effectiveness. The paper directly addresses known failure modes in GFN training, particularly relevant to red-teaming where reward signals are inherently noisy and variable. It extends prior work on GFN-based adversarial search by incorporating techniques from robust optimization and reward learning, responding to the specific challenge that unstable rewards accelerate GFN collapse in security contexts.
enables: Stable-GFN makes more reliable red-teaming workflows feasible at scale, potentially enabling security teams to systematically discover diverse vulnerabilities in deployed LLMs with greater consistency. The elimination of Z-estimation and improved robustness to noisy rewards could extend GFN applicability beyond red-teaming to other domains requiring stable distribution matching with unreliable signals. The stabilization techniques offer a methodological foundation for improving GFN training more broadly, suggesting future work on principled solutions to mode collapse. The demonstrated diversity-effectiveness tradeoff resolution supports development of more comprehensive LLM safety evaluation frameworks that balance coverage and attack quality.
